chunks.chunk_text: Last year, I made the most money I've made in my entire life. And I did it while only publishing 10 videos. And I know those numbers don't seem to make any sense because when I was starting out on YouTube, I thought the only way to make a living was by posting at least one video a week, if not more. But last year forced me to rethink basically everything I thought I knew about YouTube monetization. So, in this video, I'm going to break down the seven income streams that brought in over $290,000 last year, what they were, what it took to build them up, what they cost to maintain, and everything you need to know before starting them yourself. We're going to cover all the nitty-gritty details that most people leave out of videos like these

chunks.title: How I Made $290,000 Making 1 Video a Month (Full Breakdown)
chunks.chunk_text: . We're going to cover all the nitty-gritty details that most people leave out of videos like these. And while not every income stream will apply to every creator, my hope is that by being transparent with you and sharing everything that I can, I can give you a better sense of what's possible and what some potential options are as you build out a YouTube business of your own. Let's get into it. First up, we have YouTube AdSense. And when most people think about making money on YouTube, this is the first thing that comes to mind. If you're not familiar, AdSense is the money you can earn from the ads that YouTube runs on your videos. Once you're monetized, YouTube will actually give you around 55% of the revenue those ads generate. But in order for that to happen, you need to be accepted into the YouTube Partner Program, which for long-form content means hitting 1,000 subscribers and 4,000 watch hours

chunks.title: How I Made $290,000 Making 1 Video a Month (Full Breakdown)
chunks.chunk_text: . But in order for that to happen, you need to be accepted into the YouTube Partner Program, which for long-form content means hitting 1,000 subscribers and 4,000 watch hours. Now, exactly how much money you make from AdSense is dependent on two main factors, your views and this metric called RPM, which stands for revenue per mill or revenue per thousand views. So, if you make a video that got 1,000 views and your RPM was $5, you would make $5 in AdSense from that video. Pretty straightforward, right? The tricky part, though, was that RPM varies a lot, anywhere from under a dollar to over $20 per thousand views. This variation mostly comes down to how valuable an impression is to advertisers. But while your overall RPM range will typically be determined by your channel's audience and niche, There are some things that you can do to help maximize your RPMs regardless of what you make videos about. For one, you could upload longer videos

chunks.title: How I Made $290,000 Making 1 Video a Month (Full Breakdown)
chunks.chunk_text: . For one, you could upload longer videos. Videos over 8 minutes long unlock the ability to have mid-roll ads or ads that run while your video is playing. The longer your video is, the more ads can run and the higher your RPM can be. Additionally, some creators I know increase their upload frequency in the last quarter of the year, specifically in December. This is because RPMs tend to be higher during that time of year when advertisers are trying to spend the rest of their annual ad budgets. Something I started doing last year to increase my own RPMs is to manually increase ad slots. In YouTube Studio, I'll add manual ad slots every 2 to 3 minutes at natural breakpoints as I'm transitioning between sections or after I finish a thought

chunks.title: How I Made $290,000 Making 1 Video a Month (Full Breakdown)
chunks.chunk_text: . In YouTube Studio, I'll add manual ad slots every 2 to 3 minutes at natural breakpoints as I'm transitioning between sections or after I finish a thought. I just make sure to avoid putting any ad slots directly before or after any sponsored integrations in my videos because if a viewer sits through an entire sponsored section only to then be hit with an ad from YouTube, it can frustrate them and cause them to click away, which can hurt my videos overall retention. Manually placing an ad slot doesn't guarantee that YouTube will place an ad there. YouTube actually has their own algorithm for determining whether or not to run an ad, but the increased ad slots gives YouTube more opportunities to serve ads and many creators, myself included, have seen an increase in their RPMs after adding in more ad slots. If you are trying to maximize your RPM, the one thing that I would really avoid doing is dramatically changing the type of content you make for the sole purpose of chasing a higher RPM

And I understand the intention behind the advice. It's meant to take off some pressure and encourage exploration. But I actually disagree with it. A bit of a spicy take, I know, but I think that following this advice sets people up to just post random videos, cross their fingers, and hope that one of them will just magically take off. And when that doesn't happen, they get discouraged, burn out, and just stop posting altogether. And even if one of those random videos does take off, it creates a different problem

There's no through line, no cohesion, so they just click away. And YouTube perceives the signal that your channel probably isn't worth recommending to many others. That's why, instead of just experimenting and hoping for the best, every new YouTube channel should start with a lane, which is a combination of two things: your audience and your value. Who are your videos for, and why do they care? What do they get from watching your videos? That value piece is so important because and I don't mean to sound harsh here, but the average YouTube video is not interesting, and no one owes you their time

chunks.title: My BIGGEST Advice for Starting and Growing a YouTube Channel
chunks.chunk_text: . I think a lot of people forget that a view isn't just a number. It's a real-life person who has intentionally chosen to spend minutes of their life watching your video. One of the most common beginner YouTube video types that exist out there is a let's play video, where someone records themselves playing a game that they love, like Minecraft, and then just uploads it. Now, I totally get the appeal. It's fun, it's easy to make, and it's probably something that they're already doing in their free time. But it's also what I call a selfish video. It may be interesting to the creator, but it's unlikely that anyone else be interested in watching a random stranger play through hours of Minecraft

chunks.title: My BIGGEST Advice for Starting and Growing a YouTube Channel
chunks.chunk_text: . But it's also what I call a selfish video. It just means that you need to level up the value. Level one could be chilling on Minecraft, which is a selfish video. We could then level that up to something like "How I survived 100 days in hardcore mode," which is better. There is more of a challenge there, more of a story, but it has been done many, many times before. So, we could level it up one more time to something like "Can I beat Minecraft without jumping?" chunks.chunk_text: . After you've chosen your starting lane, it's time to launch your channel. Don't launch your channel with one video, launch it with four. And there are two main reasons for this. First, it increases your total watch time from the very beginning, which helps you get closer to monetization. As a reminder, in order to start earning AdSense revenue from YouTube, you need to meet the baseline requirements of 1,000 subscribers and 4,000 watch hours. So, these watch hours really matter. But more importantly, launching your channel with four videos helps YouTube figure out who your videos are for, which means that the algorithm can start showing your videos to the right people faster. Here's how it works

chunks.title: My BIGGEST Advice for Starting and Growing a YouTube Channel
chunks.chunk_text: . Here's how it works. At the end of each of your launch videos, include a clear call to action pointing your viewers to the next video. If someone finds one of your videos and likes it, they're much more likely to watch another video of yours. That chain of watch time sends powerful signals to YouTube. If a viewer watches not just one, but two, or three, or even four of your videos back-to-back, YouTube picks up on that and thinks, "Okay, this type of person really likes this type of video. And while there are infinite ways to pace video, most styles fall into one of three core pacing categories. At the top, we have the high energy fast pace. This is best for archetypes that are entertaining, chaotic, or comedic. It's often achieved with faster cuts that keep the momentum high, frequent framing changes like zoomins and dynamic transitions, and the removal of unnecessary pauses, breaths, or silences to keep everything moving quickly. The result is a video that feels exciting, intense, and unpredictable

One level down, we have the conversational median pace, which tends to be a go-to choice for archetypes that are relatable, thoughtful, or educational. It's created through a mix of medium length and shortcuts to maintain engagement without feeling overwhelming, subtle zooms and transitions to keep things visually engaging without being chaotic, and more pauses for emphasis, giving ideas more room to breathe. The resulting video feels smooth, engaging, and naturally conversational. And finally, we have the immersive slow pace, which is a great fit for archetypes that are introspective, atmospheric, or more low-key. It's achieved with fewer cuts, allowing moments to feel more natural and enforced, and longer, uninterpreted shots that let the viewer settle into the experience

chunks.title: Stop Copying Big YouTubers (study these channels instead)
chunks.chunk_text: over the last year countless YouTube channels have exploded and growth creators went from barely a few thousand subscribers to hundreds of thousands seemingly overnight but what makes this even more interesting is that it all happened during a time where YouTube was supposed to be more competitive than ever so I did some digging and after closely analyzing dozens of these breakout creators I discovered something whether they realized it or not each of these creators followed a new system for growing on YouTube a three-part formula that was hiding in plain sight and it all starts with part one establishing a sticky channel strategy you see most of us know that in order for a video to get recommended on YouTube people need to click on it and watch it so naturally we focus on making videos we think people will click on and watch we'll come up with an idea we think might have potential spend countless hours scripting and filming and editing your videos to Perfection agonize over creating

chunks.chunk_text: think people will click on and watch we'll come up with an idea we think might have potential spend countless hours scripting and filming and editing your videos to Perfection agonize over creating the right title and thumbnail and then press publish but while we may think that we're doing everything right we're actually neglecting the single biggest driver of YouTube growth momentum let me show you what I mean this is breakout Creator tus H over the last year T generated over 10 million views and scaled his channel to over 130

chunks.title: for the overachievers of youtube
chunks.chunk_text: this is for the overachievers of YouTube the ones who set their sight on a goal and pour everything they can into making it happen even if that means rewrites and late nights the ones who are Relentless driven obsessed and proud of it the ones who constantly told the line between pushing yourself to achieve great things and pushing yourself off of a [Music] cliff I've been an overachiever my entire life and when I decided to start out on YouTube it was no exception I didn't want to just make videos I wanted to create a life I loved making videos I loved I wanted freedom and fun and the opportunity for self- expression and creativity so in typical overachiever fashion I inhaled everything I possibly could about the way that YouTube worked YouTube advice videos interviews courses you name it I did it and through the process I realized there was a whole lot more to making a good video than I had ever imagined it wasn't as simple as come up with a video idea film yourself edit and upload

chunks.title: for the overachievers of youtube
chunks.chunk_text: I did it and through the process I realized there was a whole lot more to making a good video than I had ever imagined it wasn't as simple as come up with a video idea film yourself edit and upload all of a sudden there was a formula for Success you needed an idea that was interesting and broadly enough to go viral you needed a title and thumbnail that were as clickable as possible you needed to nail your intro and your pacing and your storytelling and your editing the formula went on and on and on and the more I learned the longer it took me to make a video what used to take 5 hours started taking 10 then 20 then 40 I was doing everything I was supposed to do and eventually it worked video started hitting hundreds of thousands of views my channel got monetized viewer started flooding in and I was Fielding sponsorship offers left and right I was living my dream but along the way something happened the spark the creativity the energy that carried me from video to video started to fade

chunks.title: for the overachievers of youtube
chunks.chunk_text: and I was Fielding sponsorship offers left and right I was living my dream but along the way something happened the spark the creativity the energy that carried me from video to video started to fade the thrill of views kept me going for a while but before long it was pressure that took its place the pressure to to keep growing keep going viral and to make each video better than the last this pressure paralyzed me I couldn't release a video unless it checked all the boxes unless it was perfect I didn't trust myself to venture outside the scope of the formula I wasn't making videos for myself anymore I was making videos for performance and as the pressure Rose the excitement the magic the spark of YouTube creation dwindled away it wasn't long before I realized that something I had worked so hard to build something that I was supposed to love had instead turned into a job I dreaded and the thing is I wasn't alone and feeling this way the single biggest reason why people give up on

chunks.title: for the overachievers of youtube
chunks.chunk_text: needs space to thrive and the more rules we impose on ourselves the more rigid we get the harder it is to even get going in the first place I spent 3 months working on my last video because it needed to be perfect and while I don't necessarily regret spending that time because I really am so proud of that video I won't do it again I've been down that road before and I can't let myself fall back into that pattern of thinking because tunneling on Perfection leads us to miss out on so many new ideas and opportunities and often times the most novel and successful videos are the result from exploring small offshoots away from the beaten path and isn't that what YouTube is all about isn't that the whole point of why we're here not to chase Perfection or fit a mold but instead to learn and to grow and to experiment to discover what we contribute and where we fit in to the Ever Changing amalgamation that is YouTube and to shape a little corner of it just for ourselves to create a life we love
